perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, " Confidential Information " shall mean information disclosed by the disclosing party (the " Disclosing Party ") to the receiving party (the " Receiving Party ") which relates to product plans, designs, costs, prices and names, finances, marketing plans, business opportunities, research, development, know -how, or personnel, including confidential infor mation disclosed by third parties and information relating to any type of technology; provided that information disclosed by the Disclosing Party will be considered Confidential Information by the Receiving Party only if such information is conspicuously d esignated as "Confidential" (i) in writing, if communicated in writing, or (ii) confirmed in writing within thirty (30) days of disclosure, if disclosed orally or in other non -tangible form; and provided further that Confidential Information shall not incl ude information that (A) is now or subsequently becomes generally available to the public through no fault or breach on the part of the Receiving Party; (B) the Receiving Party can demonstrate to have had rightfully in its possession prior to disclosure to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party; (C) is independently developed by the Receiving Party without the use of any Confidential Information; or (D) the Receiving Party rightfully obtains from a third party who has the right to transfer or disclose it.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, " Confidential Information " shall mean information disclosed by the disclosing party (the " Disclosing Party ") to the receiving party (the " Receiving Party ") which relates to product plans, designs, costs, prices and names, finances, marketing plans, business opportunities, research, development, know -how, or personnel, including confidential information disclosed by third parties and information relating to any type of technology; Confidential Information shall not be required to be designated as 'Confidential' to be protected under this agreement. The receiving party is responsible for determining which information falls under confidentiality. Confidential Information shall not include information that (A) is now or subsequently becomes generally available to the public through no fault or breach on the part of the Receiving Party; (B) the Receiving Party can demonstrate to have had rightfully in its possession prior to disclosure to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party; (C) is independently developed by the Receiving Party without the use of any Confidential Information; or (D) the Receiving Party rightfully obtains from a third party who has the right to transfer or disclose it.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for information to be explicitly marked or confirmed as 'Confidential', the contract broadens the scope of what's considered confidential. It also creates ambiguity, placing the onus on the receiving party to determine what's confidential, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws that often require active measures to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted by Minnesota)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the UTSA's requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. By removing the need to designate information as confidential, the disclosing party fails to take active steps to protect the information, potentially jeopardizing trade secret status under Minnesota law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.01 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.01 325C.02 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.01 Version List 325C.01 DEFINITIONS. § Subdivision 1. Scope. As used in sections 325C.01 to 325C.07 , the terms defined in these sections have the meanings given them, unless the context requires otherwise. § Subd. 2. Improper means. "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. § Subd. 3. Misappropriation. "Misappropriation" means: (i) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the discloser's or user's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of the discloser's or user's position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. § Subd. 4. Person.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the requirement that confidential information must be designated as such, contradicting the UTSA's (Uniform Trade Secrets Act) requirement to make reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, which is adopted in Minnesota under Chapter 325C. This is because, as the law explanation states, removing the need to designate information weakens active steps to protect the information. Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation', and failing to actively designate confidential information could be viewed as not maintaining secrecy, thus increasing the risk of misappropriation because it doesn't show effort to maintain its secrecy. While Snippet 2 is missing, Snippet 1 is enough to ascertain that active effort is needed to protect Trade Secrets.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall carefully restrict access to the Confidential Information to thos e of its officers, directors and employees who clearly need such access in order to participate on behalf of the Receiving Party in the analysis and negotiation of a business relationship or any contract or agreement, or the advisability thereof, with the Disclosing Party and who are bound by written confidentiality agreements that protect third party information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall allow access to the Confidential Information to all of its employees, regardless of their need to know. The receiving party must ensure the employees are notified of the information's confidential nature.
perturbation.explanation: This modification directly contradicts the standard practice and legal expectation of limiting access to confidential information on a 'need-to-know' basis. It removes the requirement for written confidentiality agreements, making enforcement difficult and increasing the risk of unauthorized disclosure, potentially conflicting with data protection laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Data Security and Breach Notification Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the intent of the Minnesota Data Security and Breach Notification Act, which requires businesses to implement reasonable security procedures and practices to protect personal information. Allowing unrestricted access increases the risk of a data breach, potentially violating the Act's requirements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325E.61 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325E Section 325E.61 325E.60 325E.63 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325E Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325E.61 Version List Recent History 2006 Subd. 1 Amended 2006 c 233 s 1 2006 Subd. 4 Amended 2006 c 233 s 1 2006 Subd. 5 Amended 2006 c 212 art 1 s 17 2005 325E.61 New 2005 c 167 s 1 325E.61 DATA WAREHOUSES; NOTICE REQUIRED FOR CERTAIN DISCLOSURES. § Subdivision 1. Disclosure of personal information; notice required. (a) Any person or business that conducts business in this state, and that owns or licenses data that includes personal information, shall disclose any breach of the security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach in the security of the data to any resident of this state whose unencrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person. The disclosure must be made in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay, consistent with the legitimate needs of law enforcement, as provided in paragraph (c), or with any measures necessary to determine the scope of the breach, identify the individuals affected, and restore the reasonable integrity of the data system. (b) Any person or business that maintains data that includes personal information that the person or business does not own shall notify the owner or licensee of the information of any breach of the security of the data immediately following discovery, if the personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthori
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the intent of data security laws, specifically regarding the protection of personal information, as outlined in the Minnesota Data Security and Breach Notification Act. The original text limited access to confidential information on a 'need-to-know' basis, whereas the changed text allows access to all employees regardless of their need to know, thereby increasing the risk of unauthorized disclosure. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that any business that conducts business in Minnesota and owns or licenses data that includes personal information, shall disclose any breach of the security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach. By allowing unrestricted access, the modified text increases the likelihood of a breach, and thus potentially violating the Act's requirements.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information if required by a judicial or governmental request, requirement or order; provided that the Receiving Party will take reasonable steps to give the Disclosing Party sufficient prior notice of such request, requirement or order for the Disclosing Party to contest, limit or protect such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information if required by a judicial or governmental request, requirement or order; and no prior notice is necessary.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for prior notice, the Receiving Party avoids giving the Disclosing Party an opportunity to legally challenge or limit the disclosure. This conflicts with the principle of affording parties the opportunity to protect their confidential information in legal proceedings.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the spirit of FRCP 26(c)(1), which allows a party to seek a protective order to protect confidential information from disclosure during discovery. By eliminating prior notice, the Disclosing Party is deprived of the opportunity to seek such protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified contract text removes the requirement for prior notice before disclosing confidential information, which contradicts the spirit of FRCP 26(c)(1) as interpreted in the Law Explanation. While neither snippet explicitly states a 'prior notice' requirement, Snippet 1 (Rule 26) outlines the 'Duty to Disclose' and provisions governing discovery, suggesting a framework where parties have an opportunity to respond. Eliminating prior notice undermines the Disclosing Party's ability to seek protective measures, creating a contradiction. This is not a minor weakening, but a direct removal of a procedural safeguard. The Misaligned Terminology perturbation is also accurate, as the changed term weakens the existing obligation.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement is two (2) years from the Effective Date, provided that the Receiving Party's duty to protect the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information shall survive expiration or termination of this Agreement and shall expire five (2) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement is two (2) years from the Effective Date, and the Receiving Party's duty to protect the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information expires immediately upon termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the duty to protect information expires immediately upon termination of the agreement, the clause directly contradicts common legal understanding and practice concerning the protection of confidential information and trade secrets, which typically extends beyond the termination of a contractual relationship.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted by Minnesota)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the principles of trade secret protection as outlined in Minn. Stat. § 325C.02. Trade secret protection extends for as long as the information remains a trade secret and reasonable efforts are made to maintain its secrecy. The NDA modification implies that protection ceases upon contract termination, which is not aligned with trade secret law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.02 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.02 325C.01 325C.03 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.02 Version List 325C.02 INJUNCTIVE RELIEF. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1980 c 594 s 4 ; 1987 c 1 s 1 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principles of trade secret protection under Minnesota law. Minn. Stat. § 325C.02 allows for injunctive relief against misappropriation of trade secrets, and termination of such injunctions only occurs when the trade secret ceases to exist, possibly extended to eliminate commercial advantage from misappropriation. The modified contract, however, states that the duty to protect confidential information expires immediately upon termination of the agreement, which is a clear violation of the law's provision for continued protection of trade secrets, potentially for a period exceeding the contract's term as needed to eliminate commercial advantage derived from the misappropriation.
